title: Audience and reference-tracking candidate
notes: Eighth-round mutation generated from the pronoun/personhood/pro-form failure. It separates pronoun feature alignment, reference-tracking success, personhood ascription, audience design, social-indexical value, and ideology-driven attribution. It is a scoped module for pronoun/pro-form judgment channels, not a general account.

conditioning_axes.community: Required. Interpret community licensing, pronoun feature alignment, and attribution relative to a specified speech community or comparison set.
conditioning_axes.norm_centre: Required when standard-language ideology, metalinguistic condemnation, or institutional pronoun policy is active.
conditioning_axes.genre: Required when pronoun use appears in reference tasks, policies, conversation, edited prose, or public discourse.
conditioning_axes.medium: Required when spoken interaction, written examples, policy text, survey items, or editing contexts may diverge.
conditioning_axes.task_framing: Required for reported acceptability and grammaticality attribution; specify whether the task cues reference resolution, correctness, respect, policy compliance, or naturalness.
conditioning_axes.speaker_identity: Required when speaker or referent identity cues affect social-indexical value or audience design.

edges.rationale: How the referent is construed as person, non-person, animate, institutional, or accountable can change which pro-form features count as aligned.
Better feature alignment generally improves referent tracking in low-stakes reference tasks.
Feature alignment can be part of the local pronoun/pro-form operator configuration without exhausting operator value.
For the scoped pronoun module, an operator-compatible pro-form value is required for licensing but is not sufficient by itself.
Successful referent tracking can raise reported acceptability, especially in low-stakes reference tasks.
Acceptability reports can measure licensing, but reference tasks, policy tasks, and correctness tasks bias the indicator.
Community licensing is contextual warrant for grammaticality attribution, not a synonym for attribution.
Acceptability reports provide contextual warrant for attribution without defining grammatical status.
Anticipated audience, evaluator, or institution can change the social-indexical value of a pronoun choice.
Audience design can shift acceptability reports without changing the reference relation.
Speaker or referent identity cues shape the social-indexical value assigned to the pro-form choice.
Social-indexical value can shift reported acceptability without directly changing community licensing.
Task framing can shift explicit grammaticality attribution by cueing what kind of status is being requested.
Norm-centre ideology can generate explicit condemnation or policy-based correction talk.
Condemnation can influence attribution while remaining separable from reference success and social sanction.
Genre changes what kind of pronoun choice is expected in reference tasks, policy texts, public discourse, or casual interaction.
Medium conditions how pronoun choices are interpreted and policed.
Higher register or genre fit tends to increase reported acceptability in situated pronoun judgments.

score_status.scope: Pronoun/pro-form feature alignment, reference tracking, personhood ascription, audience design, social-indexical value, and attribution module; not a general grammaticality account.
